q: The CEO insists feature X be top of the Product Backlog. The PO has customer evidence that X delivers little user value, but the CEO references strategic context the PO doesn't have full visibility into. What should the PO do?
options: Comply — the CEO has organizational context that outranks the PO's local view
Move feature X to the top quietly to avoid friction; explain the change only if asked
Surface the customer evidence, ask the CEO for the strategic context, and decide once both are clear
Maintain the current ordering by user-value evidence and decline to move feature X
why: The PO is accountable for maximizing value, and decides ordering — but maximization requires the best available evidence, not just locally observed user value. Strategic context the PO lacks may genuinely change the value calculus. Quietly complying breaks transparency; reflexively declining ignores legitimate organizational input. The empirical move is to surface both inputs before deciding.

q: The PO has spent six months refining the backlog meticulously: every item is detailed, sized, and clearly ordered. Stakeholders are pleased with the visibility. But Sprint output has dropped because Developers wait for super-detailed items and rarely shape them themselves. What should the PO do?
options: Continue — thorough refinement is the hallmark of an effective Product Owner
Delegate the writing of detailed items to the Developers
Reduce the level of upfront detail and allow more emergence and shaping during Sprints
Cancel the current backlog and rebuild it from scratch with the Developers
why: The Product Backlog is meant to emerge — items at the top are more refined, items further down are not. Over-specification turns it into a fixed plan and starves the Developers of the discovery work that creates shared understanding. Delegating writing doesn't solve over-specification. Rebuilding wastes the structure already in place. The fix is to dial back upfront detail.

q: After four Sprints toward the current Product Goal, an emerging market opportunity is significantly more aligned with company strategy. Replacing the Goal would discard most of what has been built so far. What should the PO do?
options: Stay on the current Goal; pivots after four Sprints waste prior investment and team momentum
Replace the Goal — empirical product management demands following the strongest available evidence
Inspect whether the new evidence makes the current Goal genuinely obsolete; replace it only if so
Pursue the current Goal and the new opportunity in parallel as two simultaneous Product Goals
why: A Product Goal is a long-term commitment, but not immovable; it is replaced when obsolete. 'More aligned' is not the same as 'current Goal is obsolete' — the right step is inspection. Sticking to the current Goal ignores evidence; reflexively pivoting can be premature. Pursuing two Goals at once violates the rule of one Product Goal at a time.

q: Stakeholders demand a precise scope-and-date commitment for a major release. Empirical data shows a wide confidence range. The PO should:
options: Provide the most likely date — stakeholders need a single number they can plan against
Share the wide confidence range and decline to commit to any single date publicly
Pick a date in the upper end of the range and commit to that as the official release
Share the range plus decision points for when and how the PO and stakeholders will reassess
why: Empirical forecasting under uncertainty produces ranges. Single-date commitments hide variability and create false certainty. But a bare range is hard to act on. The strongest empirical answer adds decision points — what evidence will narrow the range, and when — so stakeholders can plan around the uncertainty rather than against a fictional point.

q: The PO has shipped a deliberately small MVP to test a hypothesis. Initial customer signal is mixed: some segments respond strongly, others not at all. Stakeholders push to "finish" the feature with the originally planned full scope. The PO should:
options: Ship the originally planned full scope — stakeholders are aligned and momentum matters
Run further small experiments targeting the segments that responded, before scaling
Drop the feature given mixed signal
Increase scope but cut quality so the rest of the feature can ship faster
why: An MVP is a learning instrument: mixed signal is itself information. Scaling to full scope ignores the segmentation finding. Dropping the feature throws away the segments where it works. Cutting quality compounds the original uncertainty. Iterating where the signal is strong is what the MVP was designed to enable.

q: At Sprint Reviews, senior stakeholders dominate the conversation, propose features for the next Sprint, and the Scrum Team has had little input. The Product Owner should:
options: Let stakeholders drive — they have organizational context that the Scrum Team often lacks
Take stakeholder input but make all ordering decisions independently after the Review ends
Restructure the Sprint Review so the Scrum Team and stakeholders inspect together as collaborators
Ask the Scrum Master to gatekeep stakeholder participation in future Reviews more strictly
why: The Sprint Review is a working session where the Scrum Team and stakeholders inspect the Increment together and adapt the Product Backlog. A one-sided conversation isn't collaboration; it's a feature-request meeting. Privately ignoring input doesn't fix the event itself. Gatekeeping isn't the SM's role at the Review. Restructuring how the conversation happens is the principled fix.

q: As the product grows, leadership proposes splitting it into "domains" with a separate Product Owner for each domain to scale faster. The Product Owner should:
options: Accept — clear domain ownership reduces coordination overhead in a fast-growing product
Coach leadership on Scrum's 'one PO per product' guidance and propose alternative roles reporting to a single PO
Refuse, citing the Scrum Guide; multiple POs are not Scrum and must not be allowed
Accept with the condition that one PO be designated as 'lead Product Owner' for the product
why: At scale on one product, Scrum preserves single accountability — one PO, one Product Backlog, one Product Goal — to keep value ordering coherent. Reflexively accepting fragments the accountability. A flat refusal ignores the legitimate organizational concern. A 'lead PO' role isn't in Scrum and creates ambiguity. Coaching with workable alternatives addresses the principle and the org problem.

q: The PO is being measured by leadership on team velocity and has been pushing the team to commit to more story points each Sprint. Quality is starting to slip. What should the PO do?
options: Continue — meeting the metric is how the PO's performance is judged
Stop pushing for higher velocity and have a direct conversation with leadership about how velocity differs from value
Have the team adjust their estimation so the same work produces a higher velocity number
Resign and explain to leadership why
why: Velocity is a throughput measure, not a value measure. Pushing it at the cost of quality damages long-term Ability to Innovate and customer outcomes. Inflating estimates is gaming. Resigning over a measurement disagreement is extreme and abandons the team. The PO addresses the misaligned metric directly with leadership — that is part of how respect for PO decisions is earned.

q: A high-value initiative genuinely cannot fit into any single Sprint, even with maximum team focus. The PO should:
options: Break it into smaller, sequenced items even if the individual items each deliver narrow user value
Reject the initiative — Scrum can't accommodate work this large
Run it as a "feature project" outside the normal Scrum cadence
Find a way to break it down so each piece delivers user value, even if narrower than originally envisioned
why: Decomposition is a core Product Owner skill: large initiatives become valuable items by finding the user-value-delivering slices, even when slimmer than ideal. Sequenced items that don't independently deliver value (option 0) recreate waterfall inside Sprints. Rejecting outright (option 1) abandons the initiative. Running outside Scrum (option 2) breaks empirical product management.

q: After six months of EBM measurement, Time-to-Market has improved significantly but Current Value (NPS, retention) is flat. Leadership wants to celebrate the T2M win. The PO should:
options: Celebrate the T2M win — improved time-to-market is genuine progress for any product
Reframe with leadership: T2M without CV means the team is becoming faster at building things users don't value
Switch to metrics that better demonstrate the team's improving performance trajectory
Refocus measurement effort on Unrealized Value, where there is clearly more opportunity
why: EBM Key Value Areas are interpreted in relation to each other. T2M measures speed of delivering capability; CV measures realized value. Faster delivery of low-value work is throughput without outcome — exactly the feature-factory pattern EBM is designed to expose. Celebrating T2M alone misreads the data; switching metrics is gaming; pivoting to UV without explanation evades the inconvenient signal.
